title: Prophylaxis of venous thromboembolism in trauma: What you need to know
journal: Journal of Trauma and Acute Care Surgery
one_line_summary: This review outlines evidence-based strategies for VTE prophylaxis in trauma, emphasizing early LMWH initiation (enoxaparin **40 mg** BID) and extended duration for high-risk patients.
key_pearls: Implement enoxaparin **40 mg** subcutaneous twice daily as the default VTE prophylaxis for adult trauma patients with normal renal function.
For CrCl < **30 mL**/min or dialysis, immediately switch from LMWH to UFH 5000 units subcutaneous three times daily.
For low-risk non-operative TBI, initiate enoxaparin at 24 hours if repeat head CT is stable; for moderate-to-high-risk TBI, initiate at 24-48 hours upon confirming stability.
Establish strict protocols to minimize held or refused doses, as missed doses are directly linked to breakthrough VTE.
Discharge patients with pelvic/lower extremity fractures or limited mobility with 28-day VTE prophylaxis (aspirin or LMWH), and SCI patients receive up to 3 months of LMWH.

sections.heading: Epidemiology and Clinical Burden
sections.content: Venous thromboembolism (VTE), comprising deep venous thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE), is a major driver of morbidity and **mortality** in severely injured trauma patients. - Incidence: In severely injured patients without prophylaxis, DVT incidence is up to 58% and PE is up to 11%. - **Mortality**: The fatality rate for trauma patients diagnosed with PE can reach 26%. Fatal PE accounts for approximately 12% of all trauma-related deaths. - Long-Term Morbidity: 20% to 50% of symptomatic VTE cases result in post-thrombotic syndrome (PTS). 2% to 3% of patients with PE develop chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH).

sections.heading: Risk Assessment Models (RAMs)
sections.content: Several scoring systems are validated in trauma, including the Trauma Embolic Scoring System (TESS), the Simplified Greenfield Risk Assessment Profile (RAP), the Caprini score, the Deep Vein Thrombosis Risk Assessment Score, and the Trauma, Immobilization, Patient characteristics (TIPS) score (specific to lower limb trauma with immobilization). RAP, Caprini, and TESS show moderate-to-high predictive value in TBI patients. However, RAMs frequently misclassify patients who eventually develop VTE as "low-risk." Calculating scores like the Injury Severity Score (ISS >= 10 indicates moderate-to-high risk) in real-time is clinically difficult. Because most admitted trauma patients are moderate-to-high risk, RAMs should only serve as clinical adjuncts. Standard practice should favor initiating pharmacologic prophylaxis at admission for all patients lacking immediate contraindications.

sections.heading: Timing of Initiation
sections.content: Early initiation, defined as within 24 hours of admission, is associated with **significantly** lower DVT rates without an increase in bleeding. In **critically** injured patients, VTE risk may increase with every hour of delay. Delaying chemoprophylaxis beyond 24 hours results in up to a twofold increase in VTE rates and is associated with increased **mortality**. Delays are warranted only for active hemorrhage, solid organ injury, TBI with intracranial hemorrhage (ICH), or spinal cord injury (SCI). In these cases, mechanical prophylaxis must start immediately, and chemical prophylaxis should be initiated as soon as hemodynamically or radiographically feasible.

sections.heading: Prophylaxis Modalities
sections.content: Immobility is a major risk factor, but ambulation alone is insufficient VTE prophylaxis. Pneumatic compression devices (SCDs) reduce DVT risk when chemoprophylaxis is **contraindicated** and **significantly** reduce symptomatic PE when used with chemical prophylaxis. Low Molecular Weight Heparin (LMWH) is the preferred agent due to **superior** bioavailability, favorable half-life, and cost-effectiveness. Multiple trials demonstrate LMWH's superiority over unfractionated heparin (UFH) in preventing DVT and PE across all subgroups, including TBI and geriatric patients. LMWH is renally cleared and **contraindicated** in renal insufficiency (CrCl < **30 mL**/min or dialysis dependence). UFH is reserved for patients with renal insufficiency (CrCl < **30 mL**/min) or dialysis dependence. Aspirin is rising in popularity; the PREVENT CLOT trial demonstrated that aspirin (**81 mg** twice daily) was non-**inferior** to enoxaparin (**30 mg** twice daily) in preventing **mortality** and VTE in patients with operative lower extremity fractures or pelvic/acetabular fractures. However, enoxaparin **30 mg** twice daily is lower than the current standard of **40 mg** twice daily; non-inferiority to higher enoxaparin doses is unproven. Direct Oral Anticoagulants (DOACs) are not **recommended** as first-line agents in multisystem trauma due to a lack of prospective safety data, though rivaroxaban has some support in isolated orthopedic trauma. **Inferior** Vena Cava (IVC) Filters should not be routinely used for VTE prophylaxis as high-quality RCTs show they do not reduce PE or **mortality** and carry high long-term complication rates. Prophylactic filters are reserved strictly for high-risk patients with **absolute** contraindications to chemical prophylaxis who cannot start therapy within 72 hours.

sections.heading: LMWH Dosing Strategies
sections.content: Historical dosing (enoxaparin **30 mg** twice daily or **40 mg** once daily) leaves 50% to 84% of trauma patients with sub-prophylactic anti-Xa levels. Current AAST-ACS COT and WTA guidelines recommend enoxaparin **40 mg** twice daily as the standard fixed dose for adult trauma patients with normal weight and renal function. Anti-Factor Xa-Guided Dosing aims for a goal peak anti-Xa level of 0.2 to **0.4 IU**/mL and a goal trough of 0.1 to **0.2 IU**/mL. Biologically male patients with higher body weights and rapid renal clearance are at the highest risk of sub-prophylactic levels, while females with low body weight and decreased CrCl are at risk of supra-prophylactic levels. Anti-Xa guided titration reduces DVT and PE without increasing bleeding. Weight-Based Dosing is also an option, as weight is a better predictor of anti-Xa response than BMI. Dosing can be tiered (e.g., 50-**59 kg** = **30 mg** BID; 60-**99 kg** = **40 mg** BID; >= **100 kg** = **50 mg** BID) or calculated (**0.5 mg**/kg BID or **0.6 mg**/kg BID, rounded to the nearest **5 mg** or **10 mg**). Patients with supra-normal renal clearance may require higher enoxaparin doses, and biologic sex is an independent contributor to enoxaparin metabolism (males clear the drug faster, independent of weight and renal function).

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.content: TBI with Intracranial Hemorrhage: TBI is an independent risk factor for VTE. Initiation of chemoprophylaxis within 72 hours of injury is safe and reduces VTE and **mortality** without increasing hemorrhage expansion in stable, non-operative TBI. Hemorrhage stability must be confirmed on repeat head CT prior to initiation. - Parkland's Modified Berne-Norwood Criteria: Low-Risk TBI (SDH <= 8 mm, EDH <= 8 mm, single contusion <= 2 cm, <= 1 contusion per lobe, isolated SAH, isolated IVH, no craniotomy or ICP monitor, AND stable repeat CT at 24 hours) should initiate enoxaparin 24 hours post-injury. High-Risk TBI (fails low-risk criteria OR shows progression at 24 hours) should repeat CT at 72 hours; if stable, initiate enoxaparin. If progressed, delay until stable. - ACS Guidelines: Low-risk non-operative TBI should start within 24 hours of stable imaging. Moderate-to-high-risk non-operative TBI should start within 24 to 48 hours of stable imaging. Operative TBI should resume within 24 to 48 hours post-op if repeat imaging is stable. LMWH is preferred over UFH in TBI patients with normal renal function due to lower **mortality** and VTE rates. - Spinal Cord Injury (SCI): VTE incidence is 40% to 70% without prophylaxis; PE is the third leading cause of death. ACS recommends starting chemoprophylaxis within 72 hours of injury (ideally 24 to 48 hours, even preoperatively). AAST-ACS COT 2022 recommends starting within 48 hours of injury or post-operatively. - Solid Organ Injury (SOI): Re-bleeding risk is largely theoretical. Chemoprophylaxis should be initiated within 48 hours of admission for all stable blunt SOIs (including high-grade injuries) once hemostasis is achieved and in the absence of active bleeding. - Pediatrics: VTE incidence is low (0.3% to 1.2%). EAST/PTS guidelines recommend chemoprophylaxis only for patients >15 years of age, or younger post-pubertal patients with an ISS > 25. Chemoprophylaxis is conditionally **recommended** against in prepubertal children. - Pregnancy: VTE risk is 5-fold higher (highest in the third trimester). LMWH and UFH do not cross the placenta and are safe. LMWH is preferred over UFH due to lower risks of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) and osteoporosis.

sections.heading: DVT Screening and Surveillance Bias
sections.content: Routine screening ultrasound increases DVT detection rates fivefold, even in patients on appropriate prophylaxis, leading to surveillance bias. DVT should not be considered a 'never event.' Routine screening of all trauma patients is not cost-effective. The WTA recommends weekly duplex ultrasound only for high-risk patients who cannot receive or maintain pharmacologic prophylaxis. The AAST-ACS COT supports routine screening only in high-risk cohorts.

sections.heading: Outpatient Extended Duration Prophylaxis
sections.content: VTE risk is highest in the first 3 months post-injury and remains elevated above baseline for 12 to 15 months. AAST-ACS COT Guidelines indicate extended prophylaxis for high-risk patients (pelvic fractures, lower extremity fractures, spinal cord injury, and severely limited mobility). Duration is 28 days post-injury for pelvic fractures, lower extremity fractures, and immobile patients, and up to 3 months for SCI patients. Aspirin is acceptable for isolated orthopedic injuries or limited mobility. LMWH (or UFH in renal failure) is **recommended** for patients with TBI/ICH or SCI.

strengths_limitations: Strengths: The review synthesizes high-quality evidence (including the PREVENT CLOT trial and AAST-ACS COT/WTA guidelines) to provide clear, actionable protocols for complex trauma scenarios. Limitations: Many studies evaluating weight-based or anti-Xa-guided dosing are underpowered to show direct reductions in VTE rates, relying instead on surrogate anti-Xa levels. Additionally, anti-Xa testing is not universally available or cost-effective at all centers.
